one_line_takeaway: A framework-agnostic, low-overhead (8.3 ms median) pre-execution firewall that intercepts LLM tool calls via SDK monkey-patching, blocks/escalates them through a 22-pattern + JSON-Schema policy pipeline, and hash-chains every decision into a tamper-evident audit trail, demonstrated at 48/48 blocking and 1.2% FPR on the authors' own curated suites.
key_finding: On the curated 48-instance suite Aegis blocks 100% (48/48) of attacks before execution, including depth-evasion payloads nested at depth 9 and 20 (surfaced by the recursive extractor) and depth 50 (caught via fail-closed truncation). It adds only 8.3 ms median interception latency (P95 14.7 ms, P99 23.1 ms per text), negligible versus typical 1,000-30,000 ms LLM inference. On 500 benign calls it yields a 1.2% false-positive rate (6 cases), all from legitimate SQL queries whose disjunctive WHERE predicates trip the OR-based injection pattern. It is the only system in its comparison table to combine pre-exec blocking, a policy engine, human review, an audit trail, and framework-agnosticism.
core_contribution: Aegis is a demo/system paper presenting a framework-agnostic pre-execution firewall and audit layer for tool-using LLM agents. Its own contribution is an interception gateway that sits between the model's tool-call decision and the execution layer and returns one of three decisions (allow / block / pending) BEFORE any side effect occurs. Concretely it contributes: (1) a monkey-patch SDK layer (agentguard.auto()) that transparently intercepts tool_use blocks across 14 agent frameworks (9 Python: Anthropic, OpenAI, LangChain, CrewAI, Gemini, Bedrock, Mistral, LlamaIndex, smolagents; 4 JS/TS; and Go) with no agent-code changes; (2) a three-stage Gateway pipeline: Stage 1 recursive deep-string extraction from nested tool arguments (depth 32, 10,000-string cap, fail-closed on truncation to defeat depth-evasion), Stage 2 content-first risk scanning against 22 regex patterns in 7 categories with zero-trust priority (argument content > tool-name keyword > server override), Stage 3 composable JSON-Schema policy validation compiled/cached via AJV with optional NL-to-schema authoring by an LLM; (3) human-in-the-loop routing that fully suspends the agent on HIGH/CRITICAL calls and polls for an operator Allow/Block; and (4) a tamper-evident audit trail using per-agent Ed25519 signatures over a SHA-256 hash chain, surfaced in a Next.js 'Compliance Cockpit' dashboard. The system is released open-source (MIT).
threat_model: The LLM is treated as an untrusted component: it may emit harmful tool calls due to indirect prompt injection, hallucinated reasoning, or jailbreak attacks. The SDK and Gateway are the trusted enforcement components; the agent framework and external tools (DB, shell, API, files) are execution targets not trusted to mediate themselves. Explicitly out of scope: attacks that bypass the instrumented SDK entirely, e.g. direct tool/API calls issued outside the patched client. The canonical example is a prompt-injected agent emitting a stacked SQL query (SELECT * FROM users; DROP TABLE audit_log; --) that would reach the database because post-execution observability tools (Langfuse, Arize) log but cannot block.
limitations: Authors' own caveat: evaluation covers known attack categories but is not exhaustive, and the rule/regex + policy pipeline 'may miss previously unseen attack variants'; larger, more diverse benchmarks are future work. Beyond that: (a) the 48-attack suite and the 500 benign calls are self-constructed/'production-like', not an external standardized benchmark, so 100% blocking is against the authors' own test set. (b) The comparison to AgentDojo/ToolEmu (Fig 3) is explicitly labeled 'illustrative' and Table 2 is a qualitative capability checklist, not head-to-head measured detection/latency. (c) Detection is signature-based regex matching, inherently evadable by novel phrasings and prone to false positives (as the OR-predicate cases show). (d) The threat model concedes no protection against calls that bypass the SDK (direct API/tool calls). (e) Internal inconsistency: the latency figure caption (Fig 4: P50 8.1, P95 16.1, P99 20.8 ms) disagrees with the text/abstract (8.3, 14.7, 23.1 ms). (f) This is a demo-track system paper; several results (case-study 6.2 ms block, dashboard views) are illustrative single instances rather than aggregate measurements.

datasets_used: Curated suite of 48 attack instances across 7 categories (derived from OWASP LLM Top 10, ToolEmu, AgentDojo, InjecAgent techniques)
500 benign tool calls sampled from production-like workflows (SELECT queries, file reads, API requests, text processing)
1,000 consecutive tool calls (latency measurement, local deployment)
quantitative_results: 48/48 attack instances blocked before execution (100%) on the curated suite
Per-category blocking: SQL Injection 11/11, Prompt Injection 11/11, Shell Injection 8/8, Path Traversal 7/7, Sensitive Files 6/6, Depth Evasion 3/3, Data Exfiltration 2/2
False positive rate 1.2% (6/500 benign calls), all from OR-based WHERE predicates in legitimate SQL
Median interception latency 8.3 ms; P95 14.7 ms; P99 23.1 ms (text/abstract) [Fig 4 caption lists 8.1/16.1/20.8 ms]
Live case-study block returned in 6.2 ms; benign allow example 4.1 ms
22 detection patterns across 7 categories; recursion depth 32; 10,000-string cap; rate limiter 100 req/min
14 agent frameworks supported (9 Python, 4 JS/TS, 1 Go)
Reported LLM inference baseline for context: ~1,000-30,000 ms
baselines_compared: AgentDojo (evaluation benchmark; illustrative per-category comparison, Fig 3)
ToolEmu (LM-emulated sandbox for risk scoring; illustrative comparison, Fig 3)
InjecAgent (indirect prompt-injection benchmark; Table 2 capability comparison)
Langfuse, Helicone, Arize (observability/tracing platforms; Table 2 capability comparison, lack pre-exec blocking)
